1: Though the Lord had indeed been Abram's "shield" in the rescue of Lot and others and now reminded him of an "exceeding great reward" for his obedience (Gen. 15:1; Abr. 2:6), Abram was growing old and understandably felt that no other reward would be of lasting significance unless he received fulfillment of the promise of seed (Gen. 12:2; 13:16; Abr. 2:9-11). That seed would become the "great nation" and bless all families of the whole earth. (Ellis T. Rasmussen, A Latter-day Saint Commentary on the Old Testament, p.42)

shield (Heb. magen) defender. Symbol of God's protection for His anointed (see Psalms 84:9). Anciently, there were marks on the sheild to indicate whose tribe you belonged to. Circumcision is such a mark and Paul also talks to the Christians about a shield of faith (Ephesians 6:16).
Elder Robert D. Hales taught that God's "light will form a protective shield between you and the darkness of the adversary as you live worthy of it" (Ensign, May 2002, 70).
2: Damascus Eliezer He may have been called this because he was from Damascus, or because he led the chase to Damascus (Bereshith Rabbah 44). In Hebrew it is Dameshek, and some say it is a title given to the one in charge of a household or a teacher (Yoma 28b; cf. Amos 3:12). (Navigating the Bible)
3: According to a custom described in the Nuzi tablets from eastern Mesopotamia, one born in a man's house could become his heir. Was Abram's heir to be one born to his steward, Eliezer of Damascus? (Ellis T. Rasmussen, A Latter-day Saint Commentary on the Old Testament)
4: bowels (Heb. me‘ah) organs of procreation; place of emotions or distress or love (fig.)
7: inherit (Heb.yarash) to take possession of [same word as heir]
9: God condescends to confirm the promise of the possession of the land by a visible sign. Abraham is directed to make the usual preparations observed in old times when two parties were about to make an important covenant. Certain animals and birds used in sacrifice were slain,l and the bodies of the former divided in two parts: hence the expression to 'STRIKE' or 'CUT' a covenant.2 The two parties then passed between the two parts met in the middle, and took an oath of agreement, the position signifying that if they were false to the covenant they merited a similar fate to the animals. We must look a little closer into the manner of making a covenant in order to discover the connection of the different penalties . . . After an animal had been selected, his throat was cut across with one single blow, so as to divide the windpipe, arteries, and veins, without touching any bone. The next ceremony was to bear the breast open and pluck out the heart, and if there were the least imperfection, the body would be considered unclean. The animal was then divided into two parts, and placed north and south, that the parties to the covenant may pass between them from east to west, and the carcass was then left as a prey to voracious animals. (Revised Duncan's Ritual, pp. 113-114, by Malcolm C. Duncan.) COVENANT - The Heb BERITH is by modern scholars derived from the Babylonian BIRITY, "Binding." It was formerly explained as "cutting," referring to the custom of cutting or dividing animals in two and passing between the parts in ratifying a covenant. (The One Volume Commentary, J. R. Dummelow, p. 25.)

Examples of Israel "Cutting Covenant" with Jehovah: 1. Red Sea - between the halves of the water of the sea 2. Sinai - located on a peninsula, between the arms of the Red Sea 3. River Jordan - Israel walked between the halves of the river 4. Promised land - Israel separates in half, and declares loyalty to the law, from the two mountains, Ebal and Gerizim. 5. The Ark of the Covenant sat in the middle. A. Moses tells them what to do: Deut. 27-28 B. Joshua and Israel do it: Joshua 8:30-35 The Oath The Covenant and Oath involved both a blessing and a cursing. God promises Israel that if they broke the Covenant, He would do to them "this and more so...," meaning destruction and scattering. See Deut. 28:15, 21-32 Blindness, captivity, lose spouses and children, no Land of Promise, no inheritance, carcasses on the ground. (Typologically, no seed forever, and no inheritance in the Celestial Kingdom. No People, instead of My People.)

ALL THINGS TYPIFY CHRIST The Ancient Covenant-making Pattern 2 Nephi 11:4 "...And all things which have been given of God from the beginning of the world, unto man, are the typifying of him (Christ). Moses 6:63 "And behold, all things have their likeness, and all things are created and made to bear record of me, both things which are temporal, and things which are spiritual; things which are in the heavens above, and things which are on the earth, and things which are in the earth, and things which are under the earth, both above and beneath; all things bear record of me. THE ABRAHAMIC COVENANT Genesis 12, 15, 17; Abraham 2:6-16; Jehovah promises Abraham (1) innumerable seed, and (2) a Land of Promise for ever. It was a promise of Godhood. (D&C 132:28©33) God extends this same Covenant to all who are baptized and enter into the Covenant relationship with him, so that He becomes Our God, and we become His People.

Watch for pattern and types of Covenant making; Divisions, separations, openings, halves. What are the Covenants, and where are they made? What is the timing? Do you gain promises of "Seed forever" or a "land of Promise?" Who are you becoming? How is that being done? What is the vision you gain of the plan to "conform you to the image of Christ?" Romans 8:29 How are you like Israel on Ebal and Gerizim? Do you enter into Covenants in water, like Baptism? What are they? Do you receive the Law, like at Sinai? Is there both a blessing and a curse? Remember, "all things [in the temple] are created and made to bear record of Christ!
10: "And it came to pass, that Abram looked forth and saw the days of the Son of man, and was glad, and his soul found rest, and he believed in the Lord; and the Lord counted it unto him for righteousness." It will be noticed that according to Paul, the gospel was preached to Abraham. We would like to be informed in what name the gospel was then preached, whether it was in the name of Christ or some other name. If in any other name, was it the gospel? And if it was the gospel, and that preached in the name of Christ, had it any ordinances? If not, was it the gospel? And if it had, what were they? Our friends may say, perhaps, that there were never any ordinances except those of offering sacrifices, before the coming of Christ, and that it could not be possible for the gospel to have been administered while the sacrifices of blood were. But we will recollect that Abraham offered sacrifice and notwithstanding this had the gospel preached to him. That the offering of sacrifice was only to point the mind forward to Christ we infer from these remarkable words of his to the Jews: "Your father Abraham rejoiced to see my day: and he saw it, and was glad" [John 8:56]. So, then, because the ancients offered sacrifice it did not hinder their hearing the gospel but served, as we said before, to open their eyes and enable them to look forward to the time of the coming of the Savior, and to rejoice in his redemption. (Joseph Smith, Evening and Morning Star, March 1834, 143)

say, "May the Lord do to me this and moreso if I break my covenant with you." Indicating that if I don't keep the covenant or contract, I will be cut asunder, just like that calf or kid. Watch the "cutting covenant" pattern throughout the scriptures. You will find two things, with the covenant in between. Watch what happens. After Israel cuts covenant in the Red Sea, they go into the Promised Land through the Jordan river parting, and in the middle, the ark of the covenant is set in the middle, and they pass through on dry ground. As they get in the promised land, the Lord says he wants them to go to Shechem, right between mounts Gerizim and Ebal, put the ark of the covenant in the middle of them, Israel around the Ark, and put priests on both mountains, and they will read the curses of the law from Gerizim, and the blessings of the law from Ebal. Then, fascination of all, watch in Zechariah 14, when Jesus comes to the Jews, and puts his foot on the mount of Olives; it cuts in two, Christ is in the middle of it, the ultimate covenant, Israel gathers to him, the valley leads between the halves of the mountain, and Israel flees through it to the east, past the Temple Mount.

In the sacrifices of the covenant the animals were cut in two, and one or both parties passed between the pieces (Gen 15:10, 17). In Jeremiah 34:18 those who break the covenant with the Lord are told that they will be made like the calf which they cut in two and passed between its parts. This suggests that the oath which bound the parties to a covenant may have stipulated in the conditional curse that the violator should be treated like the sacrificial animal. (Thomas R. Valletta in Monte S. Nyman and Charles D. Tate, Jr., eds., Alma, the Testimony of the Word, p.226)

split them This was the way of making a covenant (Rashi). Indeed, the words b'rith (covenant) and bathar (split) appear to be closely related (cf. Radak, Sherashim, s.v. Barath). It symbolized that just as the two halves of the animal were really one, so were the two people making the covenant. Moreover, just as one side cannot live without the other, so the two cannot live without each other (Ralbag; Ikkarim 4:45). It was also seen as a malediction; anyone violating the oath would be torn asunder like the animals (Bachya). (Navigating the Bible)
11: carcass (Heb. peger) "corpse, carcass, monument, stela" from pagar "to be exhausted, be faint"
12: hidden" The plague (the ninth) of darkness in Egypt #Ex 10:21 is described as darkness "which may be felt." It covered "all the land of Egypt," so that "they saw not one another." It did not extend to the land of Goshen #Ex 10:23 When Jesus hung upon the cross #Mt 27:45 Lu 23:44 from the "sixth hour there was darkness over all the land unto the ninth hour." On Mount Sinai, Moses #Ex 20:21 "drew near unto the thick darkness where God was." This was the "thick cloud upon the mount" in which Jehovah was when he spake unto Moses there. The Lord dwelt in the cloud upon the mercy-seat #1Ki 8:12 the cloud of glory. When the psalmist #Ps 97:2 describes the inscrutable nature of God's workings among the sons of men, he says, "Clouds and darkness are round about him." God dwells in thick darkness. Darkness #Isa 13:9,10 #Mt 24:29 also is a symbol of the judgments that attend on the coming of the Lord. It is a symbol of misery and adversity #Job 18:6 #Ps 107:10 Isa 8:22 Eze 30:18 The "day of darkness" in #Joe 2:2 caused by clouds of locusts, is a symbol of the obscurity which overhangs all divine proceedings. "Works of darkness" are impure actions #Eph 5:11 "Outer darkness" refers to the darkness of the streets in the East, which are never lighted up by any public or private lamps after nightfall, in contrast with the blaze of cheerful light in the house. It is also a symbol of ignorance #Isa 9:2 60:2 #Mt 6:23 and of death #Job 10:21 17:13
deep sleep (Heb. tardemah) trance from radam "be unconscious, be in heavy sleep, fall into heavy sleep, be fast asleep"
darkness (Heb. chashekah) from chashak "to be or become dark, grow dim, be darkened, be black, be
horror (Heb. ‘eymah) terror, dread same as ‘ayom "terrible, dreadful"
13: surety (Heb. yada‘) to know One who becomes responsible for another. Christ is the surety of the better covenant #Heb 7:22 In him we have the assurance that all its provisions will be fully and faithfully carried out. Solomon warns against incautiously becoming security for another #Pr 6:1-5 11:15 17:18 20:16
16: Joshua [Jesus] will be the instrument of destroying the Amorites (the world) when their iniquity is full.
17: Heavenly fire has often been used by the Lord in giving miraculous manifestations to his prophets. (Gen. 15:17; Ex. 3:2; 24:17; 1 Ne. 1:6.) Lehi and Nephi, sons of Helaman, "were encircled about as if by fire, . . . and were not burned," as a means of protecting them in their ministry, a miracle soon expanded to embrace "about three hundred souls." (Hela. 5:23-49.) Fire from heaven encircled groups of worshipers in connection with the ministry of the resurrected Lord among the Nephites (2 Ne. 17:23-24; 19:13-14), and the Three Nephite disciples were cast into fiery furnaces and came out unharmed. (3 Ne. 28:21; 4 Ne. 32; Morm. 8: 24.) Similar protection was given Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego when Nebuchadnezzar had them cast into a fiery furnace so hot that it slew the handlers who carried out the deed. (Dan. 3.) (Bruce R. Mc Conkie, Mormon Doctrine, 2d ed., p.279)
and a burning lamp which passed between those pieces which Abram had divided.
18: First, remember that the RLDS church rejects this section of the D&C [132]. Therefore, when you reject this section, you reject verses 29-33. It is the only place in all scripture where the Abrahamic Covenant is explained. You will never figure it out unless you read those verses. Here the Lord explains it to Abraham, in the context of a revelation on Marriage. That is why it always shows up in the context of Marriage; Jacob, Isaac, etc. They are worried about who they are going to marry, because it directly influences the covenant. Notice that Abraham has a promise that he can continue to be a father and have children both in time and eternity. The patriarchs keep getting two types of blessings, which the world only sees one half. They see the blessing for all generations of time, but don't see that for eternity. The world can't see the eternal blessings. But the Lord sees it for all eternity. That is what made Abraham different from all the others around him. The ultimate revelation is that God is God and glorifies himself by having children throughout all eternity. This is the law by which God glorifies himself. He is our Father. Abraham will become like him because he has the same covenant that made God our Father. You can't find that anywhere else. It is only in the D&C. Notice in the Old Testament the name change. Abram, to Abraham. Exalted Father to Exalted Father of Nations. He has become like God, the Father. What about the other half of the covenant, the LAND? Where is the seed supposed to live forever? Cryptic language for the earth becoming the Celestial Kingdom. D&C 88:17-20. That is where God tells us that the earth will become the Celestial Kingdom, and that is when the meek and the poor of the earth inherit it. Note that Moses takes the Children of Israel into the Promised Land. The Promised Land is a small model of the Celestial Kingdom. Therefore it is important to keep the covenant on the land, because it is supposed to stay pristine and pure...for it represents the Celestial Kingdom. If they break the covenants they make with God, they will be cut off from the land. It will "separate" them. And they make the covenant by cutting the carcass, and the blood on the ground. The curse of the law of Moses, is that if you break the covenant and don't live the law of the land, which is the law of God, the land itself will "vomit you out and your carcasses will lie on the ground." Harsh covenant language, because you are polluting a model of the Celestial Kingdom. When you break the covenant and pollute the Celestial type, your carcass will have to lay on the ground. That was your deal with God. "May God do to me this and more so, if I do not keep my covenant." Then the land becomes called DESOLATE. Here's how you know that the Book of Mormon is an ancient document. No twentieth century farm boy will figure this out. When the city of Ammonihah was run over by Lamanites, after Alma prophesied that it would be, what happened? The "carcasses" were described on the ground, the fowls came and plucked the flesh, and the Nephites called it the Desolation of Nehors. There it is. The covenant curse. Mormon is trying to show that the law is still intact, and they broke the covenant. When they got the Jaredite records and found out that both groups had killed each other off, and rejected the prophets, they named the whole land northward the land of Desolation. They found carcasses (bones) on the ground. Law of Moses. (Robert Norman, 1989 Old Testament Class Notes)

Dust (Heb. adamah ) Dust one of the words from which comes the word dust is from the Hebrew root word of Adam. It represents the mortal body of flesh. It also is used to illustrate the curse for breaking covenants. When you are reading about "dust" and the "grave" what the prophets are telling you is that you are reversing the creative process, and that you are going back into chaos and dissolution. Pit, mire, mud, dust, the grave, hell, all these mean moving toward destruction and undoing what the Lord created. What happened when Adam fell? The result of transgression was death. From dust thou art, and unto dust wilt thou return. Movement into dust means movement into sin. See that the Book of Mormon uses the same concept. Lehi says to Laman and Lemuel, "wake up, and rise up out of the dust." Shake thyselves off. Because Laman and Lemuel are moving into Chaos and sin. They are being dissolved into nothingness so to speak. Sin brings the opposite of creation. So watch for this, the rising up out of the dust, or going into it. Resurrection is the most powerful symbol of rising up from the dust. That is why the Resurrection and the Restoration of Israel are often spoken of together. That is because Resurrection takes the dissolution and dust, and "recreates" it back into a body again, and gives it life. So what happens is that the power of Christ and the Atonement reverse all the affects of sin, and move us back into the light. Death results from breaking the law, and life, from coming to Christ. He takes the wages of sin Himself, and turns the destructive processes. He brings life back and recreates Eden for the faithful.

Blessings & Responsibilities Of The Abrahamic Covenant Earthly Blessings A promised land to live in (Abr. 2:6,19; Gen. 12:7; 17:8 A great posterity (Abr. 2:9-10; Gen. 12:2-3; 17:2, 4-6 The gospel of Jesus Christ and the priesthood for Abraham & his posterity (Abr. 2:2-11; Gen. 17:7) Eternal Parallels The celestial kingdom (D&C 88:17-20) Eternal marriage & eternal increase (D&C 132:19-22) Exaltation and eternal life (D&C 132:23-24) Responsibilities Help all of God's children receive the full blessings of the gospel (Abr. 2:9,11)

This is the covenant of becoming like the Father. There were three blessings to the covenant: land (a Celestial inheritance), priesthood (the power of the Father), and posterity (eternal increase). The scriptures compare Abraham's posterity to three elements: stars (premortal spirits - See Abr. 3:18), dust, and sand.
21: Ten tribes of the world are mentioned here that are scattered over the Promised Land. Ultimately, the lost ten tribes who are scattered throughout the world will be gathered into the Promised Land.
